danger_signals.visual_form: sprites of hostile entities (cats, ships, planes, balloons, submarines, barrels, wheels, horses)
danger_signals.meaning: immediate threat to player; collision causes damage or death

danger_signals.visual_form: visual effects from enemies like airborne attack patterns
danger_signals.meaning: ranged danger; requires evasion or blocking

danger_signals.visual_form: platforms, gaps, moving hazards rendered in stage tileset
danger_signals.meaning: structural danger; collision or falling results in damage/death

safety_signals.visual_form: rendered ground and walkable surface tiles
safety_signals.meaning: safe standing zone; player can traverse without falling

safety_signals.visual_form: collectible sprite objects placed in environment
safety_signals.meaning: health restoration or power-up acquisition; beneficial interaction

safety_signals.visual_form: level entry point or safe starting area
safety_signals.meaning: temporary invulnerability or safe player positioning at level start

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: diverse enemy sprite types (cats, mechanical/transport entities: ships, planes, balloons, submarines)

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: enemies spawn contextually in stage environments; no clear unified faction branding visible in source data

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: stage themes and tileset changes corresponding to Around the World in Eighty Days journey narrative
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemy types and difficulty curve escalate; new hazard patterns introduced per stage

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: character animation or sprite flashing (NES-era convention, not explicitly documented)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player movement or attack capability may be restricted; increased vulnerability window

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: enemy sprite removal or death animation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemy despawns; potential item drop at location

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: transition screen or level completion indicator
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: progression to next stage; world state resets for new environment

environmental_storytelling.example: game's stage progression follows Around the World in Eighty Days narrative arc; visual environment changes (ships, planes, balloons, submarines) reflect travel across different global contexts
specific enemy archetypes (ships, planes, submarines, barrels as cargo/obstacles) placed in thematic stages to reinforce world journey; hazard design reflects transportation/adventure context
Pero the Cat (Toei Animation mascot) serves as visual anchor for player identity; character design communicates playable entity role in narrative

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: entity moves within defined parameters; sprite animates continuously
randomization routine (RANDOM & 11B) determines 1/4 vs 3/4 behavior variance; entity has 3-hit HP pool (TEKHIT=3); timer-based despawn (TEKTIM)
physics-based or pattern-based movement; collision triggers player damage
can initiate attacks against enemies; damage received affects health state; movement restricted during knockback/hit animations

notes: Source provides limited visual documentation; primary data derives from code structure and enemy type naming conventions. Game lacks detailed faction/political system—antagonists are generic hazard types without ideological branding. Narrative context (Around the World in Eighty Days) informs environmental theming rather than explicit visual faction markers. NES technical constraints limit detailed manifestation layer. Uncompiled code reveals enemy behavior randomization and hit-point systems but not visual communication specifics.
